cards.text: Operators that must be members: {{c1::=}}, {{c2::[]}}, {{c3::()}}, and {{c4::->}}.
Operators that should be friends: {{c1::arithmetic}} (+, -, *, /), {{c2::comparison}} (==, !=, <, etc.), and {{c3::I/O}} (<<, >>).

cards.front: What operators should return *this by reference?
cards.back: Compound assignment operators (+=, -=, *=, /=), assignment operator (=), and prefix increment/decrement (++x, --x). This enables chaining and matches built-in behavior.

cards.front: What operators should return by value?
cards.back: Arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /), postfix increment/decrement (x++, x--), and other operators that create new objects. They return temporary values, not references.

cards.text: To enable deep copying for classes with dynamic memory: implement {{c1::copy constructor}}, {{c2::copy assignment}}, and {{c3::destructor}} (Rule of Three).

cards.explanation:
Common patterns:
  • Arithmetic (+): Friend, const ref params, return by value
  • Compound (+=): Member, modify *this, return *this&
  • Comparison (==): Friend, const ref params, return bool
  • I/O (<<): Friend, return stream ref
  • Assignment (=): Member, check self-assign, return *this&
  • Subscript ([]): Member, return ref, provide const version
examples: friend T operator+(const T& a, const T& b);
T& operator+=(const T& rhs) { /* modify */ return *this; }

cards.front: What's the Rule of Three/Five in C++?
cards.back: If you define a destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment, you should define all three (Rule of 3). In modern C++, also define move constructor and move assignment (Rule of 5). Or follow Rule of Zero by using smart pointers.

cards.text:
Prefix ++: return {{c1::*this}} by {{c2::reference}}.
Postfix ++: return {{c3::old value}} by {{c4::value}} (requires copy).
